mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz S-Class S350 / S400 / S450 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S-Class petrol engine typically costs £4,000–£14,000 supply only depending on generation. M276 V6 engines sit at the lower end (£4,000–£10,000), while M256 units run £5,500–£14,000. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000, with S-Class engines commanding a significant premium. Fitting adds £1,000–£2,500 depending on workshop location. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz S-Class S350 / S400 / S450 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine generation your S-Class has. The M276 (W221/W222, 2011–2017) is described as 'so good that ... Mercedes put it in everything it could between 2010-2017'. It has 'minor common faults, like oil leaks and problematic camshaft adjusters/cam followers'. The M256 (W222/W223, 2017–present) is 'considered robust and reliable', but has coolant leak vulnerabilities (subject to a major recall affecting ~8,500 UK vehicles), PCV system issues, and 48V hybrid complexity. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz S-Class S350 / S400 / S450 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: M276 camshaft adjuster and timing chain issues (rattling on startup, P0016/P0017 codes), M276 oil leaks from camshaft position sensors (can travel through wiring harness to ECU), M276 carbon build-up on intake valves (direct injection), M256 coolant leaks (subject to a major recall), M256 PCV/oil vapor separator issues, M256 48V mild-hybrid system complexity, and cylinder bore scoring (reported on some M256 engines). The M276 is generally more proven and reliable than the newer M256.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz S-Class petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold: if your S-Class is worth £20,000–£40,000 and a recon engine is £6,000–£8,000 (15–40%), it's well worth it. If the vehicle is a tired £12,000 example, the same engine exceeds 50% — get a scrap valuation first. S-Class models are luxury flagships with strong retained value — a good engine rebuild can add significant value if the rest of the car is in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz S-Class petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: W221 (2011–2013) S350 is M276.950 (3.5L V6, 306 PS); W222 (2014–2017) S400 is M276.824 (3.0L V6, 333 PS); W222 (2017–2020) S450 is M256 (3.0L I6, 362–367 PS); W223 (2020–present) S450 is M256 (3.0L I6, 367 PS). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M256 coolant leak, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M256 coolant leak is subject to a major recall affecting approximately 8,500 UK vehicles. Repairs are free at authorised Mercedes dealers. The recall identifier is RB-67-2022/23 and targets vehicles delivered between December 2020 and September 2023. If you notice any warning signs (sweet-smelling vapour, unexplained coolant drops, white residue near hoses), stop driving immediately and check your vehicle using the DVSA recall checker. Continued operation can destroy your engine in under 20 minutes.

eraMap.title: The S-CLASS PETROL THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: 306 PS — naturally aspirated V6, oil leaks and camshaft adjusters are the main concerns
333 PS — bi-turbo V6, direct injection, carbon build-up, cam adjusters and oil leaks
362–367 PS — first straight-six since M104, 48V EQ Boost, coolant recalls
367 PS — revised M256, same coolant leak vulnerability

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz S-Class S350 / S400 / S450 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market rates for S-Class petrol engines. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000, with S-Class engines commanding a significant premium. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only for standard units, but S-Class-specific M276 and M256 engines are substantially more expensive. M276 units generally sit at the lower end of the range; M256 units command a premium due to the 48V mild-hybrid complexity. Main dealer new engines are significantly more expensive.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an S-Class petrol engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The M276 V6 requires more labour than simpler engines; the M256's 48V system, electric eBooster and complex cooling circuit add substantial additional time.

cta.label: → Compare S-Class S350 / S400 / S450 petrol engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: M276 Camshaft Adjuster & Timing Chain Wear (W221/W222 2011–2017)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting 2–5 seconds, ticking noise, engine warning light, rough running, poor performance. The M276 has "problematic camshaft adjusters/cam followers". Timing chain stretch or wear can cause rattling and poor timing performance. The camshaft position sensor can also leak oil into the wiring harness, potentially causing ECU and electrical failures.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — timing chain stretch can occur with prolonged use and insufficient maintenance.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£4,000 (camshaft adjusters, timing chain, tensioners, sensor seals).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on startup, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The M276's camshaft adjusters and timing chain issues are well-documented. Regular oil changes with the correct MB specification are essential. If you hear rattling on startup, book an inspection immediately — preventative repair is far cheaper than a full engine rebuild. Also check for oil leaks from camshaft position sensors, which can travel through the wiring harness to the ECU.

problems.title: M256 Coolant Leak (W222/W223 S450, 2017–present)
problems.symptoms: Sweet-smelling vapour from vents or engine bay, unexplained drops in coolant level, white crystalline residue near hoses or coolant rails, dashboard overheating warnings, erratic temperature gauge behaviour. The coolant leak recall affects approximately 8,500 UK vehicles delivered between December 2020 and September 2023.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — the recall affects vehicles manufactured before October 2023.
problems.repairCost: Covered under recall — free at authorised dealers. If out of warranty, £1,000–£3,000+ depending on damage. Continued operation can destroy the engine in under 20 minutes.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you spot any of these signs, stop driving immediately.

problems.recommendation: The M256's coolant leak is a serious safety issue. Improper sealing materials degrade prematurely under high temperatures, allowing coolant to escape into sensitive areas. When seals fail, coolant can contaminate sensors, corrode wiring, or cause overheating that warps cylinder heads. Check if your vehicle is affected using the DVSA recall checker. If you notice any warning signs, stop driving immediately and book an inspection.

problems.title: M276 Carbon Build-up on Intake Valves (W221/W222 2011–2017)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, reduced performance, poor fuel economy. Direct injection engines like the M276 are prone to carbon accumulation on the intake valves.
problems.typicalMileage: 40,000–80,000 miles — accelerated by short journeys and poor-quality fuel.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, performance will continue to degrade.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: Carbon build-up is a well-known issue on direct-injection engines like the M276. The intake valves can become coated with carbon deposits, restricting airflow and causing rough running. Periodic walnut blasting of the intake valves is the most effective solution. Using high-quality fuel and occasional spirited driving can help reduce the rate of build-up.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: S-Class S350 / S400 / S450 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Walnut blasting — preventative maintenance every 40k–80k miles
Preventative — catch before ECU contamination or belt damage
Free at authorised dealers — check DVSA recall checker
Full engine replacement required if bottom-end or valvetrain fails

buyingChecks.title: Buying This S-Class Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * W221/W222 M276 with full service history and no cold-start rattle — a robust and proven V6
* W222/W223 M256 with coolant recall completed and no warning signs — a smooth and powerful I6
* M276 with camshaft sensor oil leaks already addressed
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
avoidIf: * M276 with cold-start rattle — camshaft adjuster or timing chain issues
* M276 with oil leaks or burning oil smell — sensor or timing cover leaks
* M256 with coolant loss or sweet smell — coolant leak recall may not have been addressed
* M256 with 48V system warnings — complex and expensive to repair
* Poor service history on any generation — these engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The S-Class petrol range spans two generations of Mercedes flagship engines — the M276 V6 (W221/W222, 2011–2017) and the M256 I6 (W222/W223, 2017–present). The M276 is described as "so good that ... Mercedes put it in everything it could between 2010-2017". It has "minor common faults, like oil leaks and problematic camshaft adjusters/cam followers". The M256 is "considered robust and reliable", but has coolant leak vulnerabilities (subject to a major recall affecting ~8,500 UK vehicles), PCV system issues, and 48V hybrid complexity. With the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold, a £6,000–£8,000 reconditioned engine on an S-Class valued at £20,000–£40,000 is 15–40% of the car's value — well worth it for a well-maintained example. On a sub-£12,000 example, think carefully. S-Class models are luxury flagships with strong retained value — a good engine rebuild can add significant value if the rest of the car is in good condition.

cta.label: → Compare S-Class S350 / S400 / S450 petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz S-Class S350 / S400 / S450 Petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling on startup (M276 camshaft adjuster/tensioner warning)
* Coolant Level — Check for unexplained drops or sweet smell (M256 coolant leak recall)
* Service History — Regular oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles using MB-approved oil
* Oil Leaks — Check around camshaft position sensors and timing covers (M276 common)
* 48V System — Check for any hybrid system warnings (M256 EQ-Boost)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* Recall Check — Has the M256 coolant recall (RB-67-2022/23) been completed?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

items.value: Single turbo, twin-scroll, plus BorgWarner eBooster

items.label: * 48V System
items.value: EQ-Boost mild hybrid with integrated starter-generator
